content: Which one of the following is an intensive property of a thermodynamic system?
explanation:
Density is an intensive property of a thermodynamic system. Intensive properties are those that do not depend on the amount of matter or the size of the system. They remain unchanged when the system is subdivided.

In contrast :

Mass (Option A), Energy (Option C), and Volume (Option D) are extensive properties. Extensive properties depend on the amount of matter in the system.

So, among the given options :

Density (Option B) is the correct answer, as it remains constant regardless of the system size or the quantity of material present.

Explanation

Intensive properties are used to describe characteristics of a system that are intrinsic to the material :

Density ($\rho$) is defined as the mass per unit volume of a substance :

$$ \rho = \frac{m}{V} $$

Here, $m$ is mass and $V$ is volume. Therefore, density remains the same regardless of how much of the substance there is.

Conversely, extensive properties scale with the size of the system :

Mass ($m$), a direct measure of the amount of matter.

Energy ($E$), which depends on the total amount of matter and the specific conditions.

Volume ($V$), indicating the three-dimensional space occupied by the matter.

By understanding the difference between intensive and extensive properties, it becomes clear why density qualifies as an intensive property.

content: The molar specific heat at constant volume of an ideal gas is equal to $$2.5$$ times the universal gas constant ($$8.314$$ $$J/mol.K$$). When the temperature increases by $$100$$ $$K$$, the change in molar specific enthalpy is _______________ $$J/mol.$$
The internal energy of an ideal gas is a function of

The Vander-waals equation of state is $$\left( {p + {a \over {{v^2}}}} \right)\left( {v - b} \right) = RT,$$ where $$p$$ is pressure, $$v$$ is specific volume, $$T$$ is temperature and $$R$$ is characteristic gas constant. The $$S{\rm I}$$ unit of a is
The specific heats of an ideal gas depend on its
The definition of 1 K as per the internationally accepted temperature scale is

options.content: $$1/{100^{th}}$$ the difference between normal boiling point and normal freezing point of water.
$$1/{273.15^{th}}$$ the normal freezing point of water
$$100$$ times the difference between the triple point of water and the normal freezing point of water.
$$1/{273.16^{th}}$$ of the triple point of water.

content: A body of weight $$100$$ $$N$$ falls freely a vertical distance of $$50m$$.The atmospheric drag force is $$0.5N.$$ For the body, the work interaction is

content:
Consider a mixture of two ideal gases, X and Y, with molar masses M̅X = 10 kg/kmol and M̅Y = 20 kg/kmol, respectively, in a container. The total pressure in the container is 100 kPa, the total volume of the container is 10 m3 and the temperature of the contents of the container is 300 K. If the mass of gas-X in the container is 2 kg, then the mass of gas-Y in the container is ____ kg. (Rounded off to one decimal place)

Assume that the universal gas constant is 8314 J kmol-1K-1.
explanation:
Given:

The scenario involves a mixture of two ideal gases, X and Y, with the following parameters:

  • Total pressure in the container ($ P_t $): 100 kPa

  • Total volume of the container ($ V $): 10 m³

  • Temperature of the contents ($ T $): 300 K

  • Molar mass of Gas X ($ \bar{M}_X $): 10 kg/kmol

  • Molar mass of Gas Y ($ \bar{M}_Y $): 20 kg/kmol

  • Mass of Gas X ($ m_X $): 2 kg

The universal gas constant ($ R $) is 8314 J kmol⁻¹ K⁻¹.

Using the ideal gas law, $ PV = nRT $:

$ 100 \times 10 = n \times 8.314 \times 300 $

Solving for ($ n $), the total number of moles:

$ n = \frac{100 \times 10}{8.314 \times 300} = 0.4 $

The total moles ($ n $) is the sum of the moles of gases X and Y:

$ n = n_X + n_Y $

Expressing in terms of mass and molar mass:

$ n = \frac{m_X}{\bar{M}_X} + \frac{m_Y}{\bar{M}_Y} $

Substituting the known values:

$ 0.4 = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{m_Y}{20} $

Solving for the mass of Gas Y ($ m_Y $):

$ 0.4 = 0.2 + \frac{m_Y}{20} $

$ \frac{m_Y}{20} = 0.4 - 0.2 $

$ \frac{m_Y}{20} = 0.2 $

$ m_Y = 4 \text{ kg} $

Thus, the mass of gas Y in the container is 4 kg.
